title: Microsoft Says Copilot Is 'For Entertainment Purposes Only' in Its Own Terms of Service
summary: Microsoft's terms of service quietly classify Copilot as entertainment, not a reliable tool — a surprising admission for a product deeply embedded in professional workflows. This highlights a growing tension: AI companies heavily market their tools for serious work while legally distancing themselves from the consequences of errors. For designers and developers using Copilot daily, it's a useful reminder to stay critical of AI outputs.

title: Anthropic Effectively Bans OpenClaw from Using Claude Subscriptions
summary: Anthropic has changed its policy so that Claude Code subscribers can no longer use their subscription allowance with OpenClaw, a popular third-party agentic coding tool — users must now pay extra. This signals Anthropic tightening control over how Claude is accessed through third-party wrappers, which could reshape the ecosystem of tools built on top of Claude. Developers who've integrated Claude into their workflows via OpenClaw will need to rethink their setup.

title: OpenClaw Had a Major Security Flaw That Let Attackers Gain Admin Access
summary: The popular AI agentic coding tool OpenClaw had a critical vulnerability that allowed attackers to silently gain unauthenticated admin access to users' machines. This is a significant red flag for developers relying on agentic AI tools that have deep system access. It underscores why security vetting matters just as much as capability when choosing AI-powered dev tools.

title: Anthropic Acquires Biotech Startup Coefficient Bio for $400 Million
summary: Anthropic has bought stealth biotech AI startup Coefficient Bio in a $400 million stock deal, marking a major move beyond language models into life sciences. This suggests Anthropic is positioning Claude's capabilities for specialized scientific domains, not just general-purpose use. It's a sign that leading AI labs are increasingly pursuing vertical integration into high-stakes industries.

title: AI Benchmarks Are Broken — Here's What Should Replace Them
summary: MIT Technology Review argues that the standard way of evaluating AI — pitting models against humans on isolated tasks — is fundamentally flawed and no longer reflects how AI is actually used. As models plateau on traditional benchmarks, new evaluation frameworks are needed that measure real-world usefulness, collaboration, and reliability. For anyone choosing AI tools for their work, this is a helpful lens for thinking critically about model comparisons.

title: Gemini in Google Maps Actually Makes Trip Planning Better
summary: A hands-on look at Google Maps' new Gemini integration finds it surprisingly useful for planning days out — suggesting itineraries, surfacing relevant places, and answering conversational questions about locations. Unlike Gemini's sometimes clunky presence in Gmail, this use case feels genuinely natural and well-suited to how people actually use Maps. It's a good example of AI integration that enhances a product rather than just being bolted on.

title: Proving Your Creative Work Is Human-Made Is Getting Harder
summary: As generative AI gets better at mimicking human creativity, artists, writers, and designers are increasingly facing accusations that their original work is AI-generated. This piece explores the emerging challenge of credentialing human authorship — and the platforms and tools trying to solve it. For product designers, it raises real questions about how to protect and communicate the authenticity of their work.

title: AI Health Tools Are Booming — But Do They Actually Work?
summary: Microsoft, Amazon, and OpenAI have all launched medical AI tools in recent months, but a closer look reveals significant gaps in evidence for their real-world effectiveness. The article examines how quickly these products are being deployed despite limited clinical validation. For anyone watching AI move into high-stakes domains, this is a useful reality check on the gap between marketing and proven outcomes.

title: Granola's AI Note-Taking App Makes Your Notes Public by Default — And Uses Them for Training
summary: Popular AI meeting notes app Granola quietly makes notes viewable to anyone with a link and uses them for internal AI training unless users manually opt out — despite claiming notes are private by default. This is a critical heads-up for designers and product teams who may be capturing sensitive client or project discussions in the app. It's another reminder to audit privacy settings on any AI tool that touches your work.

title: Utah Is Letting an AI System Prescribe Psychiatric Drugs Without a Doctor
summary: Utah has become only the second US state to grant a clinical AI system the authority to prescribe psychiatric medication without direct physician involvement, aiming to address healthcare shortages and reduce costs. Doctors are raising serious concerns about transparency and safety, while officials see it as a necessary experiment. It's one of the most consequential real-world deployments of AI decision-making authority to date, and a preview of debates that will intensify across many industries.
